one_line_takeaway: Encrypting only the ViT [CLS] token under CKKS makes HE-based federated histopathology learning communication-practical (~326 KB/sample, 90.02% encrypted accuracy) and blocks image-reconstruction attacks, but the evaluation is small-scale and the '30x-vs-gradient' framing conflates feature tokens with raw-image/gradient encryption.
key_finding: Sharing unprotected features/gradients is catastrophically leaky: Ridge-regression model inversion reconstructs lung-cancer histopathology images near-perfectly (avg PSNR 52.26 dB, SSIM 0.999, NMI 0.741; one image reaches 72.66 dB / SSIM 1.000). Applying CKKS to the [CLS] tokens drops reconstruction PSNR below ~20 dB (random-noise level). The [CLS]-encryption design cuts per-sample communication ~30x (326.4 KB vs 9,794.1 KB, and 1.07 GB vs 32.1 GB per aggregation round for 1,092 samples) while enabling practical server-side encrypted inference (66 ms/image, 15.2 img/s). Accuracy under encryption is 90.02% (vs 96.12% unencrypted [CLS] upper bound; a 6.10 pp drop), and encrypted [CLS] beats the encrypted-gradient baseline (85.35%) by ~4.67 pp.
core_contribution: The paper proposes a privacy-preserving federated learning (FL) pipeline for multi-institutional histopathology classification that combines a Vision Transformer (ViT) with CKKS homomorphic encryption. Its central and distinguishing idea is to make the ViT's compact 768-dimensional [CLS] token (rather than raw images or full gradients) the unit of secure aggregation: each client extracts the per-sample [CLS] token, encrypts it under CKKS, and uploads only ciphertexts. The server then does homomorphic averaging of encrypted [CLS] tokens and runs a lightweight global classifier with a degree-2 polynomial softmax approximation entirely on ciphertexts (server-side encrypted inference), so plaintext features never leave the client and never appear on the server. The authors' own claimed novelty over prior work (Related Work notes FedAvg/CNN FL, DLG/GradInversion attacks, TransUNet, BatchCrypt/CKKS) is specifically the use of the ViT [CLS] token as a low-dimensional encrypted aggregation primitive to make HE-based FL communication-practical.
threat_model: Honest-but-curious central aggregation server (and network observers) that receives client uploads and could mount reconstruction/model-inversion attacks to recover patient histopathology images from shared model updates or features. The motivation is HIPAA/GDPR prohibition on sharing raw patient data plus documented gradient-leakage attacks (DLG, inverting-gradients) that recover training samples. The paper formalizes three privacy constraints (Eq. 1): (C1) raw images x never leave the client, (C2) only encrypted [CLS] tokens are transmitted, (C3) the mutual information I(x; Enc(z_CLS)) = 0 for computationally bounded adversaries. Security rests on CKKS/RLWE semantic security at a claimed ~128-bit level. The model does not consider malicious/colluding servers, poisoning, or membership inference; C3 is asserted from CKKS semantic security rather than proven.
limitations: 1) The headline '30x' and the encrypted-gradient baseline conflate units: the 9,794 KB figure is the CKKS cost of encrypting a 120,000-value raw 200x200 image, and the paper uses it interchangeably for 'full gradients' and 'raw images'; the fair comparison of a per-sample feature token vs a per-round model gradient is not established. 2) The scheme is not classical gradient/weight FedAvg under encryption: the global classifier heads are averaged in plaintext (Eq. 15) and only the per-sample feature tokens are encrypted, so 'federated aggregation' of the model is not itself HE-protected. 3) Homomorphic aggregation is over 'aligned sample index j' across clients (Eq. 14), which presumes sample correspondence across independent hospitals that generally does not exist, undermining the aggregation semantics. 4) Constraint C3 (zero mutual information) is asserted, not proven, and the sub-20 dB 'protection' is only empirically stated without ciphertext-only attack detail. 5) Evaluation is narrow: a single lung-cancer histopathology dataset (3,311 images), only N=3 clients, IID-ish per-class splits, no non-IID/heterogeneity, no differential-privacy comparison, and no formal leakage bound. 6) softmax is replaced by an unnormalized degree-2 polynomial over [-5,5], so encrypted outputs are not proper probabilities and accuracy loss (6.10 pp) is attributed to approximation error without ablation.

datasets_used: Lung cancer histopathology dataset (Kaggle, andrewmvd 'Lung and Colon Cancer Histopathological Images'), 3,311 images across 3 classes: adenocarcinoma (lung_aca), normal (lung_n), squamous cell carcinoma (lung_scc), partitioned across 3 federated clients with 1,127 / 1,092 / 1,092 images
quantitative_results: Gradient/feature model-inversion leakage: avg PSNR 52.26 dB, SSIM 0.999, NMI 0.741, LPIPS ~0.000; single image lungaca1009 PSNR 72.66 dB, SSIM 1.000
CKKS on [CLS] reduces reconstruction PSNR to below 20 dB (random-noise level)
Communication per sample: [CLS] token 326.4 KB (768-D, 1 ciphertext) vs gradient/raw-image 9,794.1 KB (120,000-D, 30 chunks) = ~30x reduction
Per aggregation round (1,092 samples, 3 clients): 1.07 GB ([CLS]) vs 32.1 GB (gradient); 1.07 GB transmits in 86-171 s at 100 Mbps-1 Gbps
Encrypted [CLS] global accuracy 90.02%, F1 89.95%; unencrypted [CLS] 96.12% accuracy, 95.50% F1 (6.10 pp encryption drop)
CKKS encryption overhead: 0.73 s/epoch (6.5%) over 11.15 s avg training/epoch; total ~11.88 s/epoch

Server-side encrypted [CLS] inference: 66.0 ms/image, 15.2 img/s, 411 MB RAM (CPU-only), ~1,092 samples in ~72 s; ~36x faster than encrypted-gradient inference and ~36x slower than unencrypted [CLS] (1.8 ms)
CKKS params: N=8192, coeff modulus chain [60,40,40,60] bits, scale 2^40, ~128-bit security
baselines_compared: Encrypted Gradient (baseline secure FL): 85.35% accuracy, 84.25% F1; 9,794 KB data; server-side encrypted inference infeasible (>10 s/image)
Unencrypted Gradient (standard FL): 95.05% accuracy, 94.53% F1; 2.3 ms/image client-side inference, 435 img/s, 100 KB
Unencrypted [CLS] (upper bound): 96.12% accuracy, 95.50% F1; 1.8 ms/image, 556 img/s
Encrypted [CLS] (proposed): 90.02% accuracy, 89.95% F1; 66.0 ms/image server-side, 326 KB
